title: No Service Mesh (Gateway-Only)
description: Use ingress/API gateway and client libraries; lower complexity; fewer advanced traffic controls.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Gateway-only architecture requires API gateway infrastructure and compute for services. Min ($200): Small workload with managed API gateway (AWS API Gateway ~$50-100 for 1M requests + ~$100-150 for modest ECS/EKS compute for 2-3 services). Max ($8000): Production workload with high-availability API gateway cluster (~$2000-3000 for multi-AZ setup handling 100M+ requests), plus compute infrastructure for multiple services (~$4000-5000 for EKS/ECS with auto-scaling), and egress/data transfer costs (~$1000). Does not include service mesh control/data plane overhead (Istio/Linkerd would add $3000-10000/month), making this significantly cheaper than mesh alternatives.
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-high complexity adoption. Requires: (1) API gateway configuration and routing rules (20-30 hours), (2) implementing client-side libraries for service discovery, circuit breaking, retries, and load balancing (40-50 hours), (3) setting up observability without mesh-native distributed tracing (15-20 hours), (4) establishing governance and API standards across teams (10-15 hours). Total ~85-115 hours at $50-75/hour blended rate = $4250-8625, but with AI coding assistants reducing boilerplate client library implementation by 60-70%, bringing effective cost to ~$1500-2500. Lower than service mesh ($5000-15000) but higher than monolith ($500-800) due to distributed system patterns needed.
provenance.licenseCost: Zero licensing cost for open-source implementations. Popular gateway options are free: Kong Gateway (OSS), Nginx, Envoy Gateway, Traefik, AWS API Gateway (pay-per-use, no license), Azure API Management (consumption tier), GCP API Gateway. Client libraries (Resilience4j, Polly, Netflix OSS) are open-source. Organizations may optionally purchase commercial gateway support (Kong Enterprise $25k-100k/year, Apigee $50k-250k/year) but not required for basic pattern implementation. Estimated at $0 for typical OSS adoption.

provides.reasoning: This pattern uses an ingress/API gateway as the primary mechanism for routing external traffic to internal services, providing centralized routing logic without requiring service mesh complexity.
The pattern provides a single entry point via the API gateway for all external traffic, simplifying external-to-internal communication patterns.
Client libraries in this pattern typically provide resilience patterns like circuit breakers, retries, and timeouts at the client side, compensating for the absence of service mesh features.
This pattern provides fundamental traffic management capabilities through the gateway and client libraries, though without the advanced features of a full service mesh.
The no-mesh gateway pattern IS an API gateway — it provides centralized ingress, routing, and basic traffic management for all service-to-service and client-to-service communication.

requires.reasoning: This pattern explicitly relies on an API gateway as its primary entry point for traffic routing and management, replacing the more complex service mesh approach.
Client libraries are essential to this pattern as they handle service-to-service communication, load balancing, and resilience patterns that would otherwise be provided by a service mesh.

supports_nfr.reason: Gateway-only architecture without service mesh lacks automated failover and circuit-breaking capabilities limiting availability to two nines.
Gateway routing and client-side resilience libraries introduce minimum 50ms p95 latency overhead.
Without mesh-level traffic management, tail latencies under load cannot be optimized below 200ms p99.
Manual intervention and gateway reconfiguration without automated mesh traffic control requires at least 10 minutes RTO.
Without mesh-level request buffering and retry capabilities, in-flight request loss during failures results in 5 minute RPO.
At very high read QPS (>50K req/s), service mesh provides better observability, dynamic routing, and traffic splitting that gateway-only lacks.
Gateway-only architecture supports very high write query throughput limited only by gateway horizontal scaling.
HIPAA compliance requirements can be met with gateway-only architecture through appropriate application controls.
SOX compliance can be achieved with gateway-only architecture through appropriate audit controls.

supports_constraints.reason: Gateway-only architecture pattern is cloud-agnostic and works across all cloud providers and on-premises.
Gateway-only is infrastructure pattern independent of application programming language choice.
Pattern applies to any platform type requiring service-to-service communication through gateway.
Caching can be implemented at gateway or application level regardless of mesh absence.
Gateway-only pattern can coexist with async messaging for event-driven components.
AI inference workloads can be routed through gateway-only architecture without service mesh.
Multi-tenancy can be implemented at application or gateway level without requiring service mesh.
Batch processing workloads are compatible with gateway-only architecture for API access patterns.
Distributed transactions can be coordinated at application level using client libraries without mesh.
Real-time streaming can be handled alongside gateway-only architecture for request-response traffic.
Vector search services can be accessed through gateway routing without requiring service mesh.
Document store databases are data layer concern independent of gateway-only architecture.
Key-value stores can be used by services behind gateway without requiring service mesh.
Graph databases are data layer concern compatible with gateway-only service communication.
Time-series databases can be integrated with services using gateway-only architecture.
OLTP workloads are supported through gateway routing with client-side resilience libraries.
OLAP workloads can be routed through gateway to analytical services without requiring service mesh.

gateway_type.description: Type of gateway for external traffic. API Gateway provides request routing and API management; Ingress Controller for Kubernetes environments; Load Balancer for simple traffic distribution.

client_library_approach.description: Protocol for service-to-service communication via client libraries. REST/HTTP is simplest and most universal; gRPC offers better performance and type safety; GraphQL provides flexible data querying.

load_balancing.description: Where load balancing occurs. Gateway-level centralizes control; DNS round-robin is simplest but least flexible; client-side provides most control but requires library support.

observability_strategy.description: How to implement monitoring without service mesh. Application-level requires instrumentation in each service; gateway-only provides limited visibility; hybrid combines both for comprehensive observability.
